coredata.dc:title: Ultrasensitive cardiac troponin I antibody based nanohybrid sensor for rapid detection of human heart attack
coredata.dc:creator: Bhatnagar, Deepika
Kaur, Inderpreet
Kumar, Ashok
coredata.dc:description: An ultrasensitive cardiac troponin I antibody conjugated with graphene quantum dots (GQD) and polyamidoamine (PAMAM) nanohybrid modified gold electrode based sensor was developed for the rapid detection of heart attack (myocardial infarction) in human. Screen printed gold (Au) electrode was decorated with 4-aminothiophenol for amine functionalization of the Au surface. These amino groups were further coupled with carboxyl functionalities of GQD with EDC-NHS reaction. In order to enhance the sensitivity of the sensor, PAMAM dendrimer was successively embedded on GQD through carbodiimide coupling to provide ultra-high surface area for antibody immobilization. The activated cardiac troponin I (cTnI) monoclonal antibody was immobilized on PAMAM to form nanoprobe for sensing specific heart attack marker cTnI. Various concentrations of cardiac marker, cTnI were electrochemically measured using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) in human blood serum. The modifications on sensor surface were characterized by FTIR and AFM techniques. The sensor is highly specific to cTnI and showed negligible response to non-specific antigens. The sensitivity of the sensor was 109.23μAcm−2 μg−1 and lower limit of detection of cTnI was found 20fgmL−1.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cardiac troponin I
Graphene quantum dots
Heart attack
Myocardial infarction
PAMAM dendrimer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of GQD-PAMAM nanohybrid modified Au electrode
Electrochemical detection of cTnI on GQD-PAMAM nanohybrid modified electrode
Specificity, stability and accuracy of the sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the immobilization of the cardiac troponin I antibody (anti-cTnI) probe on the Au/GQD/PAMAM nanohybrid electrode and electrochemical detection of cTnI. SPE: Screen printed electrode (CE: counter electrode, WE: working electrode, RE: reference electrode).
ATR-IR transmission spectra of electrodes (a) Au/ATP (control), (b) Au/GQD, (c) Au/GQD/PAMAM, (d) Au/GQD/PAMAM/anti-cTnI and (e) Au/GQD/PAMAM/anti-cTnI/cTnI at frequency 650–4000cm−1.

AFM micrographs of electrodes (a) Bare Au, (b) 4-ATP treated Au, (c) Au/GQD, (d) Au/GQD/PAMAM, (e) After immobilization with probe (Au/GQD/PAMAM/anti-cTnI) and (f) After hybridization with target antigen (Au/GQD/PAMAM/anti-cTnI/cTnI).

Electrochemical response study using 5mMK3[Fe(CN)6] in 0.1M PBS, pH 7.4. (a) CV of Au/GQD/PAMAM/anti-cTnI and immune-binding with 10−6–10ng per 6μL cTnI in serum (a→i) at 0.03V/s. (b) Hyperbolic curve between relative Ip (with respect to probe) and increasing concentrations of cTnI in serum. Inset shows linear relationship between relative Ip with lowest concentrations of cTnI (S/N=4).

DPV of (a) Au/GQD/PAMAM/anti-cTnI and immune-complex formation with 10−6–10ng per 6μL cTnI in serum (a→i) at an amplitude potential of 25mV using 5mMK3[Fe(CN)6] in 0.1M PBS, pH 7.4. (b) Hyperbolic curve between relative Ip (with respect to probe) and increasing concentrations of cTnI in serum. Inset shows linear relationship between relative Ip with lowest concentrations of cTnI (S/N=4).

Specificity of the cardiac troponin I antibody conjugated nanohybrid sensor with cTnI and other possible antigens found in blood samples. The parallel bar graphs shows (a) the average relative Ip values and (b) Percent relative Ip values of DPV (with respect to the serum incubated immobilized probe) against cTnI and other possible antigens (1pg per 6μL).
Comparison of different modified electrodes for electrochemical detection of cTnI.

PDMS microchannel with an interdigitated array gold electrode
Ferrocene-modified silica nanoparticles on gold electrode
cTnI antibody conjugated nanohybrid modified gold electrode

head.ce:title: Ultrasensitive cardiac troponin I antibody based nanohybrid sensor for rapid detection of human heart attack

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CSIR-Institute of Genomics and Integrative Biology, Mall Road, Delhi, 110007, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CSIR-Institute of Genomics and Integrative Biology

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR)

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: CSIR-Central Scientific Instruments Organisation, Sector 30-C, Chandigarh,160030, India.
Corresponding author at: CSIR-Institute of Genomics and Integrative Biology, Mall Road, Delhi, 110007, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CSIR-Institute of Genomics and Integrative Biology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cardiac troponin I antibody conjugated with GQD-PAMAM nanohybrid modified Au electrode based sensor for early detection of human heart attack.
[RE: Reference Electrode (silver), WE: Working Electrode (gold), CE: Counter Electrode (gold), Ab: Antibody, Ag: Antigen].

An ultrasensitive cardiac troponin I antibody conjugated with graphene quantum dots (GQD) and polyamidoamine (PAMAM) nanohybrid modified gold electrode based sensor was developed for the rapid detection of heart attack (myocardial infarction) in human. Screen printed gold (Au) electrode was decorated with 4-aminothiophenol for amine functionalization of the Au surface. These amino groups were further coupled with carboxyl functionalities of GQD with EDC-NHS reaction. In order to enhance the sensitivity of the sensor, PAMAM dendrimer was successively embedded on GQD through carbodiimide coupling to provide ultra-high surface area for antibody immobilization. The activated cardiac troponin I (cTnI) monoclonal antibody was immobilized on PAMAM to form nanoprobe for sensing specific heart attack marker cTnI. Various concentrations of cardiac marker, cTnI were electrochemically measured using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) in human blood serum. The modifications on sensor surface were characterized by FTIR and AFM techniques. The sensor is highly specific to cTnI and showed negligible response to non-specific antigens. The sensitivity of the sensor was 109.23μAcm−2 μg−1 and lower limit of detection of cTnI was found 20fgmL−1.

ce:section.ce:para: The cardiovascular diseases (CVDs) are the key risk to global health and thus expanding needs for the choice of rapid, accurate, sensitive and reliable sensing devices for the detection of CVD [1]. CVD disorders include predominantly myocardial infarction (MI), coronary heart disease, cerebrovascular disease and rheumatic heart disease. The rapid and precise diagnosis and prognosis of MI has drawn great attention by researchers. Various prospective cardiac biomarkers have been identified for diagnosis of MI such as myoglobin, creatine kinase isoforms (CK-MB), cardiac troponins (cTn) and lactate dehydrogenase [2]. Among all, cardiac troponin-I (cTnI) is used as a gold standard marker due to its remarkable specificity and sensitivity for detection of acute MI which is released into the blood just after MI [3]. The level of cTnI increases in bloodstream drastically from reference limit 0.04ngmL−1 to >1.4ngmL−1 within 3–12h [4]. The conventional methods commonly used for monitoring the cTnI levels include sandwich immunoassay with secondary labelled antibodies, enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) and radioimmunoassay (RIA) [5–7]. However, these techniques have various limitations like low sensitivity, tedious, slow turn-around time, expensive and require sophisticated equipment which are not available in each diagnostic laboratory and hospital. Therefore, biosensors have turn out to be more acceptable due to high sensitivity, reliability, low cost and point-of-care diagnosis. Various detection methods have been reported including impedimetric, electrochemiluminescence, colorimetric, chemiresistive, electrochemical and fluorescence based methods [8–11]. Among these detection techniques, electrochemical method has been well accepted because it is simple, rapid, sensitive [12] and has been broadly used to detect various antigens [13–15]. An electrochemical sensor that detect cTnI has been available called i-STAT© cardiac troponin I (cTnI) from Abbott, USA that has excellent portability but has low detection sensitivity (0.02ngmL−1) in view of early detection [16].

Recently, various nanomaterials based detection strategies have been designed which has great potential for developing biosensors with improved sensitivity. In this regard, graphene quantum dots (GQD) that are zero-dimensional nanomaterial with lateral size less than 100nm from single to multi-layers, have prompted interest in optical as well as in electronic applications. The advantages of high surface area with larger length-to-diameter ratio (the ratio of length to thickness) and easy modification enable them in promising applications especially in electrochemical and fluorescence based biosensors [17–19]. Similarly, dendrimers (poly-amidoamine, PAMAM) are nanomaterial that significantly useful in assembling of electrochemical biosensors [20]. These are synthetic macromolecules with regularly branched tree-like and globular arrangement. It contains large number of available amine sites for conjugation that significantly contribute to the sensitivity of sensor. Thus, the exceptional features of GQD and PAMAM may be combined and further explored to develop a diagnostic tool that lead to successful detection and quantification of target analyte viz, cTnI.

Here, we have designed an electrochemical sensor for detection of cTnI using GQD-PAMAM nanohybrid modified gold (Au) screen printed electrode. The working electrode (Au) was functionalized with linker molecule 4-aminothiophenol (ATP) for amine termination. Then, GQD and PAMAM were immobilized on ATP modified electrode through chemically driven method stepwise forming Au/GQD/PAMAM electrode. A capturing monoclonal anti-troponin I antibody (anti-cTnI) was then mounted on the above electrode with carbodiimide chemistry. The target antigen, cTnI was captured on the above modified Au/GQD/PAMAM/anti-cTnI electrode through antigen-antibody interaction and change in current was measured through cyclic voltammetry (CV) and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV). The sensor was further treated with various non-specific antigens released in human blood at different physiological states. The proposed sensor was also validated with present available methods and results were compared in terms of specificity, sensitivity and limit of detection of cTnI.

Cardiac troponin I monoclonal antibody (anti-cTnI) and cardiac troponin I full length protein purchased from Abcam, Cambridge, USA. Polyamidoamine (PAMAM) dendrimer third generation (G3; Mol. Wt.-6909), 4-Aminothiophenol (ATP), 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethyl-aminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC) and N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, USA. Graphene quantum dots (GQD) obtained from ACS material, USA. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), potassium hexacyanoferrate (III) [K3Fe(CN6)], sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), disodium hydrogen phosphate (Na2HPO4), potassium di-hydrogen phosphate (KH2PO4), ethanol and other chemicals were obtained from Qualigens, India. All the reagents were analytical grade and solutions were prepared in deionized water. Screen printed gold electrodes were procured from Dropsens, Spain and modified at IGIB. Human cTnI ELISA kit was purchased from Calbiotech Inc., USA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of GQD-PAMAM nanohybrid modified Au electrode
ce:section.ce:para: The assembly of the Au/GQD/PAMAM electrode, immobilization of the anti-cTnI probe and hybridization with target cTnI to form Au/GQD/PAMAM/anti-cTnI/cTnI and its electro-chemical detection schematically represented in Scheme 1 . The screen printed Au electrode (gold as the working and counter electrodes and silver as the reference electrode) was modified for the fabrication of the GQD-PAMAM nanohybrid based cTnI sensor. The working Au electrode surface (0.126cm2) was activated with 6μL piranha solution (H2SO4: H2O2, 3:1, v/v) for 5min and then washed 4–5 times with deionized water. The electrode was dried and dehydrated with 70% ethanol. In order to terminate the Au surface with amine groups, 6μL 4-ATP solution (10mM in ethanol) was placed on the activated working Au surface (0.126cm2) for 24h followed by subsequent 3–4 times washing with de-ionized water to remove unbound 4-ATP and dried at room temperature [21]. Further 5μL equimolar mixture of 10mM EDC and NHS (1:1, v/v) in PBS (100mM sodium phosphate buffer, pH 7.4 containing 0.9% chloride) was added in 10μL GQD solution (100μgmL−1) to convert the carboxyl groups into active reactive ester [22]. Then, 6μL of the above solution was placed on the working Au electrode surface for 3h to allow the formation of amide bond between COOH groups of the GQD and NH2 groups of 4-ATP.
The electrode was washed to remove unbound GQD and similarly, 6μL of PAMAM solution (165μgmL−1) was added to form Au/GQD/PAMAM nanohybrid electrode.

The carboxyl terminal of the capturing anti-cTnI antibody probe was activated by mixing 10μL anti-cTnI solution (100μgmL−1) in PBS with 5μL of equimolar mixture of 10mM EDC and NHS (1:1, v/v) to make the final probe concentration 50μgmL−1. Then, 6μL of the above reaction mixture was immobilized on the Au/GQD/PAMAM working electrode for 3h at room temperature to allow the formation of the amide bond between the −COOH groups of anti-cTnI and – NH2 groups of PAMAM to make Au/GQD/PAMAM/anti-cTnI nanohybrid electrode. The unbound antibody were removed by washing 4–5 times with PBS, pH 7.4 and dried at room temperature before electrochemical measurements.

The electrochemical performance of the electrode was studied with CV and DPV in 5mMK3[Fe(CN)6] solution in PBS, pH 7.4 as redox indicator at a potential range from −0.2V to 0.8V with a scan rate 0.03V/s. The Au/GQD/PAMAM/anti-cTnI electrode was initially incubated with 6μL human blood serum (as control) for 10min at working electrode, washed with PBS pH 7.4, dried and measurement of CV and DPV were performed using redox indicator 50μL as described above. Further, similar measurements were carried out with different concentrations of cTnI prepared in blood serum from 10−6 to 10ng per 6μL Besides, to ensure the specificity of the sensor non-specific antigens released in human blood at different physiologic conditions were also explored on this sensor as described for DPV measurement.

The surface functionalities of ATP treated Au (as control), Au/GQD, Au/GQD/PAMAM, Au/GQD/PAMAM/anti-cTnI and Au/GQD/PAMAM/anticTnI/cTnI electrodes were determined using Attenuated total internal reflectance infrared spectroscopy (ATR-IR, BX-59333, Perkin Elmer, USA) at frequency 650–4000cm−1. The ATR-IR spectrum of Au/ATP was taken as background and subtracted from the reflectance spectra of all successive modified electrodes. The surface topology of the developed sensor was characterized by Tapping mode atomic force microscopy (AFM, Bruker MultiMode 8, Germany).
The performance of the sensor in terms of sensitivity and specificity was studied and discussed using CV and DPV measurements in the sections below.

The ATR-IR transmittance spectra were taken to confirm the surface functional groups of GQD-PAMAM nanohybrid formed on Au, after immobilization of the anti-cTnI and its binding with the cTnI on electrode (Fig. 1 ). The ATR-IR spectrum of ATP modified Au electrode is considered as control for rest of the modified electrodes. Since the interfaces were subtracted from the control value, the ATR-IR transmittance of Au/ATP electrode (Fig. 1a) shows significant peaks at 1112, 1206 and 2586cm−1 which are explicit for the CS, CN and SH stretch, respectively formed due to the attachment of ATP molecules on the gold surface [23]. Peaks at 1518, 1674, 2674–3124 and 3300–3532cm−1 were also found for stretching vibrations of NH (1° amine), CC, aromatic CH and NH (1° and 2° amine) respectively. For, Au/GQD (Fig. 1b) modified electrode, addition of carboxyl terminated GQD was confirmed with peaks at 1067, 1444 and 1729cm−1 for CO stretching, CH bending and CO stretching respectively. Amide bond formation between NH2 groups of ATP and COOH groups of GQD was also confirmed by the presence of peak at 1604cm−1 along with peaks at 1348, 2824–3120 and 3200–3537cm−1resemble for CC stretch, CH bend and NH (1° and 2° amine) stretch. The ATR-IR spectrum of Au/GQD/PAMAM shows peaks at 762, 900 and 3074–3465cm−1 for the NH stretching and peaks at 1018 and 1161 are for CN stretching.

Rest peaks at 1345, 1439, 1623, 1730 and 2905cm−1 are for CC, CC, amide, carbonyl and OH stretching vibrations (Fig. 1c). The ATR spectrum of Au/GQD/PAMAM/anti-cTnI as shown in Fig. 1d exhibits peaks at 1018 and 1164cm−1 are for 1° and 2° CN stretching, 1369 and 1443cm−1 for CH3 and CH groups, respectively confirming the presence of amino acids on anti-cTnI [24]. Peak at 1604cm−1 confirms the amide bond formation between amino-terminated PAMAM and carboxyl-terminated anti-cTnI. Rest peaks are similar for above functional groups. The peaks of Au/GQD/PAMAM/anti-cTnI/cTnI shown in Fig. 1e were similar to Au/GQD/PAMAM/anti-cTnI but with higher transmittance (10% increase) which confirms the binding of cTnI to the anti-cTnI (antigen-antibody binding).

The tapping mode AFM was used to investigate the topological features and change in roughness factor (Ra) during successive modifications of the electrode. Fig. 2 a shows surface study of bare Au which has an average Ra value of 6.76nm and after modified with 4-ATP, an average roughness increased to 14nm (Fig. 2b). But after decorated with GQD, (Fig. 2c) a densely packed surface morphology with uniform distributed granular structure was observed with Ra reduced to 6.5nm. The Ra was simultaneously increased to 8.01nm for Au/GQD/PAMAM after assembling layers of PAMAM onto it due to formation of well aligned nanohybrid layers (Fig. 2d). Immobilization the antibody on electrode further form Au/GQD/PAMAM/anti-cTnI electrode (Fig. 2e) and the surface was filled with globular structure protein molecules with an average Ra value 9.05nm. Finally, after hybridization with target antigen cTnI, the surface was observed to have 12nm Ra value that indicate the antigen-antibody interaction onto the modified Au surface (Fig. 2f).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical detection of cTnI on GQD-PAMAM nanohybrid modified electrode
ce:section.ce:para: In the absence of cTnI, the GQD-PAMAM nanohybrid modified electrode immobilized with probe shows more current response. In contrast, in the presence of cTnI, the specific cTnI antigen and anti-cTnI antibody leads to decrease in current. The oxidation peak current of CV and DPV significantly decreases with respect to the increasing concentrations of cTnI in serum. This was due to the formation of more immune-complex on working electrode surface causing decrease in current.

The CV peak current (Ip) of probe (Au/GQD/PAMAM/anti-cTnI) electrode incubated with serum was taken as control (63μA) for the various cTnI concentrations prepared in human blood serum (Fig. 3 a). The Ip of Au/GQD/PAMAM/anti-cTnI/cTnI was lower than that of Au/GQD/PAMAM/anti-cTnI and it decreases with increasing concentrations of cTnI through successive immune-complex formation. The plot between the various concentrations of cTnI and the relative Ip values with reference to the probe was hyperbolic (Fig. 3b) for cTnI following a linear equation [Ip (μA)=11524 (μAng−1)×cTnI (ng) + 18.05] and regression coefficient (R2) 0.9902 (Fig. 3b, inset). The sensitivity (S) of the cTnI sensor was 91.46×103 μAcm−2 ng−1(91.46μAcm−2 μg−1) which was calculated using the formula, S=m/A, where, m is the slope of the linear equation and A is the area of the working Au (0.126cm2) electrode. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated using the formula LOD=3 (s/S) where, s is the standard deviation and S is the sensitivity [25]. The LOD for cTnI was found 0.15 fg per 6μL (25fgmL−1).

In DPV measurements, the Au/GQD/PAMAM/anti-cTnI electrode in the absence of cTnI shows a high oxidation peak current (Ip) in comparison to the cTnI incubated electrode in which Ip value significantly decreased with increase in cTnI concentrations due to immune-complex formation (Fig. 4 a) as in CV measurements. The inset plot between cTnI concentrations and the relative Ip values with respect to the probe also shows hyperbolic curve (Fig. 4b) following the linear equation [Ip (μA)=13763 (μAng−1)×cTnI (ng)+21.93] and regression coefficient (R2) was 0.9794 (Fig. 4b, inset). The sensitivity of the sensor was 109.23×103 μAcm−2ng−1 (109.23μAcm−2 μg−1) and LOD for cTnI in serum was calculated as 0.1fg per 6μL (20fgmL−1).

The low detection limit could be achieved due to immobilization of large number of anti-cTnI molecules on available sites on large surface area of GQD-PAMAM nanohybrid Au electrode that strongly binds specific antigen cTnI.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity, stability and accuracy of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The accuracy of the sensor was checked with the already available method ELISA for cTnI (Supporting Data) which showed a good correlation (R2) 0.9547 between the two methods. The different concentrations of cTnI in serum were determined by ELISA kit (y-axis) [26] and by the present GQD-PAMAM nanohybrid based sensor (x-axis).

The known concentrations of cTnI (0.1 and 1ngmL−1 in serum) and unknown cTnI analyte (0.5 and 0.8ngmL−1 in serum) were made and real values obtained by both the methods (ELISA and present sensor) from standard curve plotted in Fig. S1 which showed a good correlation (R2 =0.9547) with linear equation (y=1.0042x). The sensitivity of ELISA method is only 0.02ngmL−1 whereas the present method has better sensitivity hence it was calibrated with lower concentrations of cTnI (fg per 6μL in serum) for early detection of cTnI level in serum heart attack patients (Fig. S2). The lower limit of detection of cTnI was found approximately 15fgmL−1 which is lower value of cTnI detection in serum as compare to all reported methods.

Hence, our sensor is ultrasensitive than other methods, which may help to diagnose patient at early stage before 3h of heart attack symptoms (chest pain etc.). The present sensor can be used in point-of-care diagnosis in hospitals and remote areas where sophisticated instruments are not available.

The cardiac troponin I antibody conjugated with GQD-PAMAM nanohybrid based sensor can detect as low as 20fgmL−1 (0.1fg per 6μL) cTnI in 10min for confirmation of the risk of myocardial infarction (heart attack). The sensitivity of the electrode sensor was 109.23μAcm−2 μg−1 with DPV and found highly specific to the cardiac biomarker troponin I. The antibody conjugated nanohybrid modified Au electrode is better than earlier reported methods due to high sensitivity (109.23μAcm−2 μg−1), ultralow detection limit (20fgmL−1), very less sample requirement (6μL), less detection time (10min) and specific only to target antigen cTnI.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Deepika Bhatnagar thanks ICMR, New Delhi for providing senior research fellowship to carry out the research work. DB also acknowledges NanoSHE (BSC0112) for financial support in chemicals and Prof. Pradeep Kumar, IGIB for scientific discussions. Blood serum samples were obtained from Nexus Central Lab Services (P) Ltd, New Delhi.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.ijbiomac.2016.11.037.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

How to interpret elevated cardiac troponin levels

Electrochemical enzyme immunoassays on microchip platforms
A new immunoassay for the measurement of myoglobin in serum

Recent development of cardiac troponin I detection
Biofunctionalized rebar graphene (f-RG) for label-free detection of cardiac marker troponin I
Developing Electrochemical Biosensors for Point-of-Care Diagnostics of Cardiovascular Biomarkers, Electronic Thesis and Dissertation Repository
Electrochemical biosensors for the determination of cardiovascular markers: a review
Aptamer biosensor for label-free impedance spectroscopy detection of proteins based on recognition-induced switching of the surface charge
An overview of label-free electrochemical protein sensors
Electrochemical detection of protein by using magnetic graphene-based target enrichment and copper nanoparticles-assisted signal amplification
Point-of-care i-STAT cardiac troponin I for assessment of patients with symptoms suggestive of acute coronary syndrome
Graphene quantum dots FRET based sensor for early detection of heart attack in human
Graphene quantum dots: versatile photoluminescence for energy, biomedical, and environmental applications
Graphene quantum dots-based platform for the fabrication of electrochemical biosensors
Electrochemical and surface characterization of 4-aminothiophenol adsorption at polycrystalline platinum electrodes
Multisubstrate-compatible ELISA procedures for rapid and high-sensitivity immunoassays
Omp85 genosensor for detection of human brain bacterial meningitis
Cardiac troponin I level in STEMI and clinical correlation with left ventricular dysfunction in Indian population
Electrochemical immunosensor for simultaneous detection of dual cardiac markers based on a poly (dimethylsiloxane)-gold nanoparticles composite microfluidic chip: a proof of principle
Electrochemical detection of cardiac biomarker troponin I at gold nanoparticle-modified ITO electrode by using open circuit potential
Label-free detection of cardiac troponin-I using carbon nanofiber based nanoelectrode arrays
Gold and silver nanoparticles for electrochemical detection of cardiac troponin I based on stripping voltammetry
Electrochemical detection of cardiac troponin I using a microchip with the surface-functionalized poly (dimethylsiloxane) channel
Polymer-modified microfluidic immunochip for enhanced electrochemical detection of troponin I
Electroactive graphene-multi-walled carbon nanotube hybrid supported impedimetric immunosensor for the detection of human cardiac troponin-I
Electrochemical aptasensor of cardiac troponin I for the early diagnosis of acute myocardial infarction
Rapid electrical immunoassay of the cardiac biomarker troponin I through dielectrophoretic concentration using imbedded electrodes
